summary: The V51.1 physical/apparent sun altitude split creates cascade failures across 12+ WINs. The firmament ceiling H(r) at the sun's orbital position is ALWAYS below 4,200 km (ranges 1,975–3,618 km through the year), making the dome's '<4,200 km' constraint meaningless. The real constraint is the local ceiling, which forces the physical sun to as low as 1,975 km at winter solstice. Refraction needed: 13–27° (vs real atmospheric refraction of ~0.03°). The split doesn't fix the collision — it reveals that the model's entire observational framework is internally inconsistent.

master_argument.title: The Physical/Apparent Split Is a Model-Wide Consistency Failure
master_argument.key_insight: The dome's firmament height H(r) = 8,537·exp(−r/8,619) decreases exponentially with disc radius. The sun orbits between r = 7,400 km (summer) and r = 12,615 km (winter). At these radii, H(r) ranges from 3,618 km to 1,975 km. The '<4,200 km' physical altitude constraint is NEVER the binding constraint — the local firmament ceiling is always lower. If the physical sun maintains constant altitude, it must be below 1,975 km (the minimum ceiling in its orbit). If it varies, the seasonal altitude change creates effects not observed.
master_argument.refraction_impossibility: To shift the apparent position from ~2,000-3,600 km (physical) to 5,733 km (apparent), refraction must bend light by 13-27° at mid-elevations. Real atmospheric refraction at 30° elevation is ~0.03°. The dome needs 400-900× real refraction. The dome's own n(r) formula gives values far too small for this displacement.
master_argument.universal_displacement: If refraction shifts the sun by 2,000-3,800 km vertically, it must also shift: the moon (at 2,534 km — would appear at ~5,000+ km), all stars (on firmament at 1,500-8,500 km depending on radius), and all triangulation baselines. Every altitude measurement in the model becomes unreliable. The parameters used to BUILD the model (including the 2,534 km moon altitude) were presumably measured through the same refractive medium — they are all 'apparent' values, making the 'physical' values unknowable.
master_argument.seasonal_modulation: The required refraction varies from 13° (summer) to 27° (winter) — a factor of 2. This seasonal refraction modulation would be visible as: (1) seasonally varying stellar positions, (2) seasonally varying angular sizes of celestial objects (83% variation in sun if physical altitude tracks ceiling), (3) seasonally varying refraction of the horizon. None of these are observed.
master_argument.angular_diameter_paradox: At physical altitude ~1,975 km with 26.5 km core radius, the sun's angular diameter from below would be 1.54° — 2.9× the observed 0.53°. The dome claims 'fully decoupled elevation and angular diameter' — this is an unfalsifiable escape hatch that removes a previously testable prediction.

affected_wins.impact: DIRECT — This WIN is about solar elevation derived from H(r)
affected_wins.cascade_effect: The dome's solar elevation formula uses the globe's 23.45° axial tilt, not arctan(H_physical/d). With the physical altitude now unknown (an inequality, not a value), the dome CANNOT derive a solar elevation formula from its own geometry at all. The formula would be arctan(H_physical(t)/d(t,φ)) where H_physical(t) is an unknown function bounded above by H(r_sun(t)). The split converts a self-contradiction into an unfalsifiable claim.
affected_wins.new_argument: V51.1 replaces the defined sun altitude (5,733 km) with an inequality (<4,200 km), but the binding constraint is actually the local firmament ceiling: H(r) = 3,618 km at summer solstice, 2,673 km at equinox, 1,975 km at winter solstice. The dome cannot derive solar elevation from geometry because the physical altitude is not a number — it's a seasonally varying upper bound.

affected_wins.impact: HIGH — Eclipse geometry depends on sun/moon positions
affected_wins.cascade_effect: Eclipse shadows are cast by physical objects at physical positions. If the physical sun is at ~3,200 km on August 12 (H(r=8,395) = 3,223 km ceiling), and the moon is at 2,534 km, the sun-moon-observer geometry changes entirely from the 5,733 km calculation. The eclipse shadow's size, umbra/penumbra ratio, and ground track all depend on the sun's actual altitude. The dome's killshot.html predicts -28.9 nT at specific stations — but which sun altitude enters this calculation? If it used 5,733 km (apparent), the shadow geometry is wrong. If it used a physical altitude, it used an undefined number (<3,223 km). The 2017 eclipse observation (the basis of WIN-010) was also analyzed with some sun altitude — was that 5,733 km? The analysis needs recalculating with the physical altitude.
affected_wins.new_argument: The V51.1 split creates an eclipse geometry paradox: shadows are cast by physical objects at physical positions, but the dome's eclipse predictions (killshot.html: -28.9 nT) were calculated using an undefined physical sun altitude. At the August 2026 eclipse date (Aug 12), the firmament ceiling is only 3,223 km — the sun must be below this, not at 5,733 km. All eclipse geometry and FSF station predictions need recalculating, but the physical altitude isn't a number.

affected_wins.impact: MEDIUM — Analemma shape depends on sun's orbital geometry
affected_wins.cascade_effect: On the dome, the analemma depends on the sun's altitude and orbital radius. If the physical altitude is not 5,733 km but varies seasonally between ~2,000-3,600 km (tracking the ceiling), the analemma traced by the physical sun is fundamentally different from the observed figure-8. The dome uses the globe formula (23.45° sinusoid) anyway, so the direct numerical impact on our existing argument is small — we already show the dome doesn't derive the analemma from dome geometry. The cascade strengthens this: the dome now CANNOT derive the analemma because the physical altitude is undefined.
affected_wins.new_argument: V51.1 makes dome-native analemma derivation impossible: the figure-8 shape depends on the sun's actual trajectory, but the physical altitude is an inequality varying with the ceiling constraint. The dome's continued use of the globe's 23.45° formula is now the only option — it literally cannot compute its own analemma.

affected_wins.impact: MEDIUM — Same as WIN-018, affects loop ratio derivation
affected_wins.cascade_effect: Same as WIN-018. The 2.66 loop ratio would change if the physical sun traces a different trajectory (varying altitude). The dome's spirograph mechanism produces 2.66 from a constant-altitude orbit; a varying-altitude orbit produces a different ratio. But the dome doesn't derive 2.66 from dome geometry anyway.
affected_wins.new_argument: Combined with WIN-018: the analemma analysis is strengthened. Add cross-reference to V51.1 cascade.

affected_wins.impact: HIGH — Tidal forcing depends on physical sun position
affected_wins.cascade_effect: Tidal forces are gravitational — they depend on the PHYSICAL position of the forcing body, not the apparent position. A physical sun at ~2,000-3,600 km (varying seasonally) is CLOSER to the disc surface than 5,733 km, making the gravitational gradient even steeper. The one-spike problem worsens: at winter solstice, the sun at <1,975 km creates an extremely concentrated forcing — a sharp thermal/gravitational pulse directly below, dropping to near-zero at 5,000 km horizontal offset. This is farther from the observed two-bulge pattern. Additionally, the 83% seasonal altitude variation predicts enormous seasonal tidal force modulation (force ∝ 1/r² or 1/r³), which is not observed.
affected_wins.new_argument: V51.1 makes tidal physics worse: the physical sun at 2,000-3,600 km is closer to the disc than the previous 5,733 km, steepening the gravitational gradient and making the one-spike problem more severe. The 83% seasonal altitude variation predicts tidal force variations of 3-8× between summer and winter — not observed. Tidal forcing depends on physical position, not apparent.

affected_wins.impact: MEDIUM — Ray geometry changes with physical sun position
affected_wins.cascade_effect: The dome argues crepuscular rays diverge from a local sun at 5,733 km. But V51.1 says the physical sun is at <3,200 km (on a typical day). Light rays from a sun at 3,200 km diverge more strongly than from 5,733 km — the apparent divergence angles increase. More critically: if the refractive medium bends light by 13-27°, 'straight' crepuscular rays would show visible curvature. Rays traveling through a medium with such extreme refraction cannot appear parallel. The anticrepuscular ray argument (convergence at the anti-solar point proves distant sun) is strengthened by the cascade: extreme refraction would prevent anticrepuscular convergence at a well-defined point.
affected_wins.new_argument: V51.1's extreme refraction (13-27°) would make crepuscular rays visibly curved, not straight. If the medium bends sunlight by 20°+ to shift apparent altitude by thousands of km, it also bends crepuscular rays — they cannot appear parallel.

affected_wins.impact: CRITICAL — Refraction inconsistency destroys all stellar position claims
affected_wins.cascade_effect: The dome's n(r) formula predicts ~0.04° of refraction at mid-latitudes for starlight. But the V51.1 sun altitude split requires 13-27° of refraction for sunlight. These are the same medium, the same observer, the same physics. Either n(r) is correct (0.04° refraction, no significant displacement of anything, sun/firmament collision remains) or the sun is genuinely displaced by thousands of km (requiring ~15°+ refraction, which would displace every star by degrees, destroying all astrometric predictions). The dome cannot have both: weak refraction for stars AND strong refraction for the sun. The claimed +0.27° Polaris 'excess' would be dwarfed by the multi-degree displacements required by the sun altitude split.
affected_wins.new_argument: V51.1 creates a fatal refraction inconsistency: the sun's physical-to-apparent displacement requires 13-27° of refraction, but the dome's n(r) formula predicts only ~0.04° for Polaris at similar geometry. The model cannot simultaneously have weak refraction (for stellar precision) and extreme refraction (for solar displacement). This single inconsistency invalidates all dome astrometry.

affected_wins.impact: MEDIUM — H(r) function used for both gravity and sun constraint
affected_wins.cascade_effect: The same H(r) function constrains both gravity (via aetheric pressure) and the sun (via firmament ceiling). At the Antarctic rim, H(r) = 837 km. The sun at winter solstice (closest to rim, r=12,615 km) must be below H(r) = 1,975 km. The model uses H(r) to explain both the gravity anomaly and the sun constraint, but the predicted gravity effect at the rim (90% loss) vastly exceeds observation (0.53% variation). The cascade is: if H(r) is wrong about gravity by a factor of 170, it's probably wrong about the firmament ceiling too — and the sun altitude constraint is meaningless.
affected_wins.new_argument: H(r) simultaneously constrains gravity and the sun's physical ceiling. If H(r) overpredicts the gravity effect by 170× at the rim, the firmament ceiling derived from H(r) is equally suspect. The V51.1 split relies on H(r) for the '<4,200 km' constraint, but H(r) is demonstrably wrong for gravity — why trust it for the firmament?

affected_wins.impact: MEDIUM — FSF derives from H(r)/r dome geometry
affected_wins.cascade_effect: The Firmament Scaling Function (FSF) uses H(r)/r ratios to predict station-specific eclipse magnetic anomalies. The V51.1 sun altitude split means the illumination geometry (sun-station-firmament angles) changes. FSF values that were calibrated assuming sun at 5,733 km would give different results with the sun at ~3,200 km. The 0.642-2.075 FSF range across stations would change, invalidating the 9-station eclipse predictions.
affected_wins.new_argument: The FSF calibration assumed sun at 5,733 km. V51.1 moves the physical sun to <3,200 km (eclipse date ceiling). All FSF station predictions need recalculating — but the dome provides no updated FSF values.

affected_wins.impact: MEDIUM — 39-eclipse ensemble used some sun altitude for geometry
affected_wins.cascade_effect: The ensemble analysis of 39 historical eclipses presumably used a consistent sun altitude for dome geometry calculations. With the physical altitude now undefined and varying seasonally, each historical eclipse would need recalculating with the appropriate seasonal ceiling. The ensemble's statistical significance depends on consistent geometry.
affected_wins.new_argument: V51.1 invalidates the 39-eclipse ensemble: each eclipse occurred at a different season with a different firmament ceiling (1,975-3,618 km), requiring different physical sun altitudes. The ensemble cannot use a single consistent geometry.

affected_wins.cascade_effect: WIN-016 argues the dome's refractive aberration model requires wavelength dependence (chromatic dispersion). The V51.1 extreme refraction (13-27°) makes this worse: any real medium with refractive index high enough to bend light by 15°+ would show enormous chromatic dispersion. Sunsets would show dramatic color separation over degrees, not the observed ~0.5° green flash. White stars would appear as rainbow smears.
affected_wins.new_argument: V51.1's extreme refraction requirement (13-27°) amplifies the chromatic dispersion problem: a medium refracting by 15°+ would split white sunlight into a rainbow spanning degrees. Not observed.
affected_wins.reason: WIN-016 argument already covers this; add cross-reference note only

affected_wins.impact: MEDIUM — Firmament wobble model relies on consistent geometry
affected_wins.cascade_effect: The 20m firmament wobble parallax model assumes known geometry between stars and the firmament. If refraction is extreme enough to displace the sun by thousands of km, it would also displace the firmament's apparent position and the apparent parallax — the 20m wobble would appear as a different value through the refractive medium.
affected_wins.new_argument: V51.1's extreme refraction would distort the apparent parallax from any firmament wobble. A 20m wobble seen through a medium with 15°+ refraction would not appear as the clean 0-0.5 arcsecond signal claimed.
affected_wins.reason: Lower priority; existing WIN-017 argument is already strong

cross_cutting_section_proposal.title: V51.1 Refraction Cascade: How One Parameter Fix Breaks Everything
cross_cutting_section_proposal.recommended_location: New subsection in Part 2 (Self-Contradictions) or as extension of kill-shot analysis
cross_cutting_section_proposal.rationale: The cascade is a single coherent argument: the physical/apparent split requires extreme refraction that contradicts the dome's own n(r) formula, destroys all astrometric precision, creates unobserved seasonal modulations, and makes every parameter in the model unreliable. This is better presented as a unified cascade argument than as scattered notes in individual WINs.
outline: 1. The split: physical <4,200 km → actually <1,975 km (ceiling at winter solstice)
2. Refraction needed: 13-27° varying seasonally (vs 0.03° real atmospheric)
3. The n(r) contradiction: dome's own formula gives ~0.04° for stars, not 15°+ for the sun
4. Universal displacement: moon, stars, all triangulation baselines are affected
5. Seasonal modulation: 83% angular diameter variation, 3-8× tidal force variation
6. Eclipse geometry paradox: shadows use physical positions, but physical altitude is undefined
7. Self-defeating fix: the split was meant to resolve sun/firmament collision but creates a model-wide inconsistency that is harder to fix than the original problem
cross_cutting_section_proposal.status: proposal — needs human approval for section creation

issues_to_create.title: V51.1 cascade: eclipse geometry uses undefined physical sun altitude
issues_to_create.description: Eclipse shadows are physical, not apparent. V51.1 moves the physical sun below 3,223 km (Aug 12 ceiling), but killshot.html predicts -28.9 nT using unstated geometry. Which altitude enters the eclipse calculation?

issues_to_create.title: V51.1 cascade: analemma derivation impossible with undefined sun altitude
issues_to_create.description: The physical sun altitude is now an inequality, not a value. The dome cannot derive an analemma from its own geometry because the trajectory is undefined. Strengthens existing argument.

issues_to_create.title: V51.1 cascade: closer physical sun worsens one-spike tidal problem
issues_to_create.description: Tidal forces depend on physical position. Sun at 2,000-3,600 km (vs 5,733) steepens the gradient. 83% seasonal altitude variation predicts 3-8× tidal force modulation not observed.

issues_to_create.title: V51.1 cascade: extreme refraction makes crepuscular rays curved
issues_to_create.description: 13-27° refraction needed for sun displacement would visibly curve all light rays through the medium. Crepuscular rays cannot appear straight or parallel in such a medium.

issues_to_create.title: V51.1 cascade: n(r) gives 0.04° for stars but sun needs 15°+ refraction
issues_to_create.description: Fatal refraction inconsistency: dome's n(r) predicts 0.04° stellar refraction but sun displacement requires 13-27°. Same medium, same physics, 400× discrepancy. Invalidates all dome astrometry.

issues_to_create.title: V51.1 cascade: H(r) unreliable for ceiling (wrong by 170× for gravity)
issues_to_create.description: H(r) overpredicts gravity variation by 170× at the rim. The same function sets the sun's physical ceiling — why trust it for firmament height if it fails for gravity?

issues_to_create.title: V51.1 cascade: FSF calibration invalidated by changed sun altitude
issues_to_create.description: FSF uses H(r)/r geometry assuming sun at 5,733 km. Physical sun at <3,200 km on eclipse day changes all station-specific predictions. No updated FSF values provided.

issues_to_create.title: V51.1 cascade: 39-eclipse ensemble needs per-season recalculation
issues_to_create.description: Each historical eclipse occurred at different season with different ceiling (1,975-3,618 km). Ensemble cannot use consistent geometry with the V51.1 split.

note_015_response: Cascade analysis completed. Traced V51.1 sun altitude split through 12 WINs (8 Steve requested + 4 additional). Created 8 new issues for the decider. Proposed cross-cutting section for unified cascade argument. Key finding: the '<4,200 km' constraint is never the binding constraint — the local firmament ceiling is always lower (1,975-3,618 km in the sun's orbital range). This forces the physical sun to ~2,000 km at winter, requiring 27° refraction to appear at 5,733 km. The dome's own n(r) formula predicts only 0.04° — a 675× discrepancy.
